coredata.dc:title: Isolation, structural characterization of (1 → 4), (1 → 6)-α-D-glucan from Dioscorea Alata and synthesis and catalytic activity studies of (1 → 4), (1 → 6)-α-D-glucan derived carbon dots

coredata.dc:creator: Mallik, Arnab
Patra, Sukesh
Patra, Animesh
Pandey, Akhil
Hazra, Madhumita
Sahoo, Gobinda Prasad
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a straightforward, productive protocol was adopted for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) by a simple microwave-assisted technique from (1 → 4), (1 → 6)-α-D-glucan polysaccharide (DAPS). The isolation and structural characterization of (1 → 4), (1 → 6)-α-D-glucan from the aqueous extracting of the Diascorea Alata was described here. The photo-physical and morphological studies of the prepared high quantum yield (27.70 %) CDs were systematically characterized using different analytical techniques: TEM, DLS, XPS, XRD, FT-IR, TCSPC, EDX, Fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy. In our study the carbon dots (CDs) obtained are non-toxic, highly water-soluble, spherical-shaped negatively charged particles with an average diameter of 3.28 nm. The photo-catalytic activity of carbon dots (CDs) was tested under the sunlight for photo-degradation of crystal violet (CV), a pollutant organic dye. Carbon dots (CDs) showed outstanding results regarding the photo degradation of CV (achieving 99.9 % degradation within 8 min) under sunlight.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polysaccharides
Glucan
CDs
Dye degradation
Crystal violet

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Isolation and purification of the polysaccharide
Isolation, purification and structural characterization of the DAPS
Morphological and compositional descriptions of as-prepared carbon dots (CDs)
Mechanism of dye degradation by using synthesized carbon dots (CDs) as photocatalysts

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: University Grants Commission South Eastern Regional Office

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The instruments facilities obtained from the economic support of Rashtriya Uchchatar Shiksha Abhiyan (RUSA) (Ref. No. 620/MC/RUSA2.0/PROJECT/19/13(11); Dated: March 14, 2019), MHRD, Department of Higher Education, Govt. of India, New Delhi and Research Center (Science), Midnapore College (Autonomous) to carry out the investigation is gratefully acknowledged. We are thankful to Prof. Ajay Misra, Dept. of Chemistry & Chemical Tech. Vidyasagar University, Midnapore, W. B. India, for allowing us to do the TCSPC measurement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) 1H NMR spectrum (500 MHz, D2O, 30 °C) of glucan, isolated from Diascorea alata. (b) 13C NMR spectrum (125 MHz, D2O, 30 °C) of polysaccharide, isolated from Diascorea alata.
(a) HSQC spectrum of the polysaccharide isolated from Diascorea alata. (b) Part of the HMBC spectrum of polysaccharide, isolated from Diascorea alata. The delay time in the HMBC experiment was 80 ms.

(a) TEM image (Scale bar is 50 nm) of carbon dots (CDs) using polysaccharide, isolated from Diascorea alata. (b) Histogram of size distribution of carbon dots (CDs). (c) DLS measurement for size distribution of carbon dots (CDs). (d) EDX spectra of carbon dots (CDs). (e) Zeta Potential curve of carbon dots (CDs).

(a) Carbon dots (CDs) UV–vis spectrum. (b) Fluorescence emission profile of carbon dots (CDs) at excitation wavelength is 280 nm (emission wavelength starts at 310 nm and end wavelength at 510 nm, excitation and emission slit is 5,5 and emission maximum at 368 nm. (c) TCSPC measurement of carbon dots (CDs). (d) Photographic images carbon dots (CDs) solution with and without irradiation of UV light.
(a) Survey XPS spectrum (Percentage of elements shown in inset),Deconvoluted core-level XP spectrum of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s and (d) N 1s.
FTIR spectra of synthesized carbon dots (CDs).
XRD spectrum of carbon dots (CDs) synthesized using polysaccharide, isolated from Dioscorea alata.
FL intensity of carbon dots (CDs) at (a) different PH (0–14) (b) at different concentration of NaCl (0.1 M–1 M) (c) Time under UV light (d) storage time for stability checking.

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectra of CV aqueous solutions in catalyst's [carbon dots (CDs)] presence at different degradation times, (b) Emission profile of CV aqueous solutions in catalyst's [carbon dots (CDs)] presence at different degradation times, (c) kinetic plot of the degradation of the CV and (d) Photographic images in the degradation pathway of the CV aqueous solutions with the carbon dots (CDs). Effect of carbon dots (CDs) in sunlight on CV violet color (left side violet cuvette) to colorless (transparent solution in right cuvette). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic flow diagram of isolation and purification of polysaccharides.
Schematic illustration of the mechanism for toxic CV dye degradation using carbon dots (CDs) as photocatalyst.
The 1H and13C NMR chemical shifts for the glucan isolated from Diascorea alata in D2O at 30 °C.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a Values of the 1H chemical shifts were recorded with respect to the HOD signal fixed at δ 4.74 at 30 °C.
b Values of the 13C chemical shifts were recorded with reference to acetone as the internal standard and fixed at δ 31.05 at 30 °C.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The significant J H,C connectivities observed in an HMBC spectrum for the anomeric protons/carbons of the glucan isolated from Diascorea alata.

head.ce:title: Isolation, structural characterization of (1 → 4), (1 → 6)-α-D-glucan from Dioscorea Alata and synthesis and catalytic activity studies of (1 → 4), (1 → 6)-α-D-glucan derived carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry (UG and PG), Midnapore College (Autonomous), Midnapore, 721101, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry (UG and PG), Midnapore College (Autonomous), Midnapore- 721101, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Botany, Midnapore College (Autonomous), Midnapore, 721101, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Botany, Midnapore College (Autonomous), Midnapore-721101, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, PRMS Mahavidyalaya, Jamboni, Bankura, 722150, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, PRMS Mahabidyalaya, Jamboni, Bankura- 722150, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a straightforward, productive protocol was adopted for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) by a simple microwave-assisted technique from (1 → 4), (1 → 6)-α-D-glucan polysaccharide (DAPS). The isolation and structural characterization of (1 → 4), (1 → 6)-α-D-glucan from the aqueous extracting of the Diascorea Alata was described here. The photo-physical and morphological studies of the prepared high quantum yield (27.70 %) CDs were systematically characterized using different analytical techniques: TEM, DLS, XPS, XRD, FT-IR, TCSPC, EDX, Fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy. In our study the carbon dots (CDs) obtained are non-toxic, highly water-soluble, spherical-shaped negatively charged particles with an average diameter of 3.28 nm. The photo-catalytic activity of carbon dots (CDs) was tested under the sunlight for photo-degradation of crystal violet (CV), a pollutant organic dye. Carbon dots (CDs) showed outstanding results regarding the photo degradation of CV (achieving 99.9 % degradation within 8 min) under sunlight.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: During the last two decades, carbon dots (CDs) has gained enormous scientific importance due to their exceptional and novel properties which include excellent water solubility [1], photo stability [2], low toxicity [3] and magnificent photo luminescence properties [4,5]. These exceptional properties have unlocked a new gateway for their application in sensors [6], optoelectronic devices [7], waste water treatment, catalysis [8] and cell labelling [9]. Several natural sources are employed to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) such as leaves [10], flowers [11], fruits [12], food waste [13], mushroom [14], DNA [15], etc. by using environment-friendly and low-cost synthetic strategy. Polysaccharides, including cellulose and starch, being non-toxic, easily available and biodegradable are utilized successfully for preparing carbon dots (CDs) using different methods that include pyrolysis using phosphoric acid [16], reaction with NaOH or HCl hydro-thermally [17], hydrothermal treatment with Poly[imino(1,2-ethanediyl)] [18] and hydrothermal process using microwave-assisted technique in water [19]. Among the above-mentioned method, hydrothermal process using microwave-assisted technique is a simple, time-saving, cost-effective method for preparing carbon dots (CDs) and consumes low energy in comparison to other conventional hydrothermal treatment. DAPS belongs to α-D-glucan polysaccharide family and is isolated from Dioscorea Alata which is an easily available food material. Now enough raw materials required for the fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) in large scale can easily be achieved by this DAPS. Shen et al. [20] has utilized sweet potato, a food material, for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) previously. As we are mainly interested in resources that are economically viable, easily available, sustainable, environmentally-safe and nontoxic, so, we first isolate a water soluble polysaccharide (1 → 4), (1 → 6)-α-D-glucan (DAPS) from Dioscorea Alata and synthesize fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from this DAPS using an aqueous solution of sulfuric acid in a simple, facile, efficient and sustainable technique through microwave-assisted approach. The structure of the repeating unit of DAPS was determined based on acid hydrolysis, methylation analysis, periodate oxidation, and NMR experiments (1H, 13C, TOCSY, DQF-COSY, HSQC and HMBC).

Recently, due to rapid industrialization and urbanization, toxic synthetic dye-stuffs are released from various manufacturing sectors like textile, beauty, painting, food and ink industries and this creates a matter of concern as it disturbs aquatic ecosystem and may be a source of mutagens and carcinogens in human life [21]. Therefore, before the disposal of such toxic dye-stuffs into the earth's surface, their appropriate treatment is necessary to minimize environmental pollution. But, it is the biggest challenge to decolorize dye-stuffs in waste water due to its ineffectiveness towards oxidants, light and heat here [22].

Among all the toxic industrial discharges, one of the extensively used synthetic dye is a triphenyl methane dye, crystal violet. This dye is well known to cause allergy, eye irritation, complication in central nervous system and kidney disorders [23,24]. So, the proper treatment and removal of this toxic dye from waste water streams have gained a huge research interest. Although their removal is tough due to the complexity in organic structure, many methods have been developed including bio-degradation [25], adsorption [26], flocculation [27], ultra-filtration [28] etc. for appropriate treatment of CV dye in waste water. The existing processes for the treatment of dye are very expensive and technically inefficient, therefore can't be applied successfully. So, there is an essential need for the development of reliable, cost-effective and eco-friendly method for the proper treatment of dye-stuffs from waste water for the safety of human beings as well as the environment. In this purpose, we endeavour a lot to synthesize a cost-effective photo-catalyst for effective degradation of CV dye. Carbon dots (CDs) being non-toxic, easily available, low cost material becomes the best candidate for photo-catalytic degradation of CV dye. Here, when sunlight falls in the carbon dots (CDs), there is an excitation of electron from VB to CB. That excited electron then react with O2 to produce super-oxide radical and the holes created in VB react with H2O to form hydroxyl radical. These highly energetic radicals attack the organic pollutant CV dye in waste water and as a result CV dye is converted to CO2, H2O and harmless by products.

In this work, we utilized the synthesized carbon dots (CDs) from natural source plant fraction (DAPS) for the toxic dye CV degradation under sunlight. The prepared carbon dots (CDs) show excellent degradation properties. We believe that our work is unique as (i) We utilize an isolated water soluble, non-toxic, economically viable, easily available, environmentally benign pure polysaccharide α-D-glucan (DAPS) first time to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) in a simple and cost-effective route with high quantum yield (27.70 %) and (ii) the synthesized carbon dots (CDs) show excellent emission, good water solubility, PH independence and outstanding result regarding photo-degradation of CV (achieving 99.9 % degradation within 8 min) under sunlight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation and purification of the polysaccharide
ce:section.ce:para: The collection of yam tuber of Diascorea alata (about 1 kg) was done from the Midnapore local market, West Bengal, India and thoroughly washed with water. Cleaned tuber was cut into fine slices, boiled at 100 °C with double-distilled H2O for 6 h and then cooled. The supernatant was precipitated in 1:5 (v/v) ethanol subsequently filtration through liner cloth followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 45 min. After centrifugation, the precipitate was collected and dissolved in the smallest amount of distilled H2O. The whole solution was dialyzed against double distilled H2O for 24 h using cellulose membrane (D9652, Sigma-Aldrich, retaining MW > 12,400 Da) and lyophilized to obtain crude polysaccharide (1.2 g). Crude water-soluble polysaccharide (25 mg) was fractionated by gel permeation chromatography on a column (90 cm × 2.1 cm) of Sepharose-6B fitted with RediFrac fraction collector using distilled H2O as eluent. The eluents collected in the test tubes were assayed for carbohydrate by the phenol-sulfuric acid method [29]. Pure homogeneous polysaccharide fraction (DAPS) was freeze-dried and collected (16 mg). The purification procedure was carried out regularly. Total purification process is shown in Scheme 1 .

The molecular weight of DAPS was determined by gel-chromatographic technique [30,31]. Standard dextrans T-200, T-70, and T-40 were passed through a sepharose-6B column, and then the elution volumes were plotted against the logarithms of their respective molecular weights. The elution volume of DAPS was then plotted in the same graph, and the molecular weight of DAPS was determined.
The optical rotation of the DAPS was measured on a Jasco Polarimeter model P-1020 at 30 °C.

Paper partition chromatographic studies were performed using Whatmann nos.1 and 3 mm sheets [32]. Solvent systems used were: (X) BuOH-HOAc-H2O (v/v/v, 4:1:5, upper phase) and (Y) EtOAc-pyridine-H2O (v/v/v, 8:2:1). Alkaline silver nitrate solution was used as a spray reagent.

Hydrolysis of DAPS (3.0 mg) was carried out with 2 M CF3COOH (2 mL) at 100 °C for 18 h. The excess acid was removed, hydrolyzate was reduced with NaBH4, acidified with dilute CH3COOH, and then co-distilled with pure CH3OH. The reduced sugars were acetylated with 1:1 pyridine-acetic anhydride (100 °C for 1 h). GLC analysis of the alditol acetates was done by HP 5730 A with FID and glass columns (1.8 m × 6 mm) packed with 3 % ECNSS-M (A) on Gas Chrom Q (100–120 mesh) and 1 % OV-225 (B) on Gas Chrom Q (100–120 mesh). The temperature was maintained at 170 °C throughout the GLC analyses.

To prepare the carboxymethyl reduced polysaccharide the DAPS (3.5 mg) was dissolved in 1.0 M imidazole-hydrochloric acid buffer, pH 7.0 (200 μL/mg) and cooled on ice. Sodium borohydride (40 mg) was then added and reacted on ice for at least 1 h. The excess borohydride was destroyed by adding glacial acetic acid (100 μL/40 mg borohydride) slowly to the cooled sample. An equal volume of distilled water was then added, and the reduced polysaccharide was precipitated by adding three times of the volume of 95 % (v/v) ethanol. The sample was re-precipitated two more times with 95 % ethanol and the residue was freeze-dried. The carboxyl reduced polysaccharide was divided into two portions [33]. One portion was hydrolyzed with 2.0 M CF3COOH for 16 h at 100 °C. After removing excess acid, the hydrolyzed product was reduced with NaBH4 and finally acetylated with 1:1 pyridine-Ac2O to yield alditol acetate and analyzed by GLC. Another portion was subjected to a periodate oxidation experiment.

DAPS (3.0 mg) was methylated using the procedure described by Ciucanu and Kerek method [34]. The methylated product was then hydrolyzed with 1 mL 90 % formic acid (100 °C, 1 h). Hydrolyzate was reduced with sodium borohydride, acetylated with pyridine-acetic anhydride (1:1), and analyzed by GLC-MS on a Shimadzu QP-2010 instrument equipped with a ZB-5MS capillary column (30 m × 0.25 mm) using a temperature gradient of 150 °C(hold time 5 min) to 200 °C at 2 °C/min. A portion of the methylated DAPS (2.0 mg) was dissolved in dry THF (2 mL), refluxed with LiAlH4 (40 mg) [35] for 5 h and kept overnight at room temperature. The excess of the reductant was decomposed by dropwise addition of EtOAc and aqueous THF. The inorganic materials were filtered off. The carboxyl-reduced methylated material was hydrolyzed with formic acid as before. The alditol acetate of the carboxyl-reduced, methylated sugar was prepared in the usual way and analyzed by GLC-MS.

DAPS (20 mg) was oxidized with 0.1 M sodium metaperiodate solution (20 mL) in the dark at 25 °C for 3 days. Ethylene glycol was added to destroy the excess periodate, followed by dialysis against distilled water for 2 h. It was then reduced with NaBH4, acidified with dilute CH3COOH, and then co-distilled with pure CH3OH. The periodate-reduced material was divided into two parts. One part was hydrolyzed, acetylated as the process mentioned above, and the alditol acetate was analyzed by GLC. Another part was methylated and alditol acetates were kept for GLC–MS analysis.

The absolute configuration of monosaccharide was determined by Gerwig et al. [36]. In brief, DAPS (1.0 mg) was hydrolyzed with CF3COOH and dried for removal of unreacted acid. The hydrolyzate was then mixed with 250 μl of 0.625 M HCl solution treated with R- (+)-2-butanol and the whole mixture was heated at 80 °C for 16 h. After removal of the reactants, it was treated with N, O-bis (trimethylsilyl) trifluroacetamide (BSTFA) to prepare TMS-derivatives which were then analyzed by GLC using a capillary column SPB-1 (30 m × 0.26 mm) with a temperature program (3 °C/min) from 150 to 210 °C. The 2,3,4,6-tetra-O-TMS-(+)-2-butylglycosides derived from DAPS were compared with those prepared from D and L enantiomers of different monosaccharides and the absolute configuration of DAPS was determined.

DAPS was dried over P2O5 in a vacuum for several days and then exchanged with deuterium followed by lyophilizing with D2O (99.96 % atom 2H, Aldrich) four times. The 1H and 13C NMR experiments were carried out at 500 MHz and 125 MHz, respectively with a Bruker Avance DPX-500 spectrometer. The 1H, 13C, TOCSY, DQF-COSY, HSQC and HMBC NMR spectra were recorded in D2O at 30 °C. The 1H NMR spectrum was recorded by suppressing the HOD signal (fixed at δ 4.70) using the WEFT pulse sequence. Acetone was used as an internal standard (δ 31.05 ppm) for the 13C spectrum. The 2D-DQF-COSY experiment was performed using standard BRUKER software. The TOCSY experiment was recorded at mixing time of 150 ms, and the complete assignment required several TOCSY experiments having mixing times ranging from 60 to 300 ms. The NOESY mixing delay was 300 ms. The HMBC mixing delay was 80 ms.

The carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized using an easy one-step microwave-assisted approach. About 0.1175 gm fine powder of previously isolated polysaccharide fraction DAPS was taken, 10 mL 2 N H2SO4 was mixed and subjected to sonication for 10 min. The whole solution was taken in a microwave for 10 min. After that, carbon dots (CDs) were separated from large carbon nanoparticles by centrifuging the mixture at 12000 rpm for 15 min. Final solution was filtered and filtrate was taken in a 25 mL stopper conical which was carbon dots (CDs) solution. Then, the sample was preserved in powder form via a freeze-drying process for characterization. About 0.05 gm of carbon dots (CDs) is synthesized from 0.1175 gm of polysaccharide (% of yield = 42.55 %) by using the 85 % power of a microwave oven of 800 W.

The quantum yield (QY) of synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) is determined by relative method using the following formula (Eq. (1))
(1) QS (%) = QR. mS / mR. nS 2 / nR 2
Where, “Q” signifies quantum yield, “n” indicates the refractive index and “m” as the slope value. The subscript “S” and “R” represent the synthesized carbon dots (CDs) and reference substance (here, quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 is taken as the reference substance).

To test the catalytic action of the carbon dots (CDs), the degradation of CV was done under sunlight in presence of carbon dots (CDs). carbon dots (CDs) were separated from large carbon nanoparticles by centrifuging the mixture and preserved as powder form. Before using as a catalyst, the powder form of carbon dots (CDs) was again dissolved in water and 100 μl of 0.02 mg/mL of these solutions was added to 5 mL of CV solution having concentration of 10 ppm. In order to record the variation in absorbance and fluorescence, UV–vis absorption and emission studies were done at a 30 s time interval respectively. As the reaction progresses, a gradual change in color from violet to green and finally to clear solution was observed. Also, the controlled experiment was carried out without using carbon dots (CDs) and both UV–vis and emission spectra of CV showed no changes with time.

UV-1800 Shimadzu spectrophotometer is used for recording UV–vis absorbance. Powdered XRD study was done in a ‘Rigaku Miniflex-II’ X-ray diffractometer using CuKα radiation (λ = 0.154056 nm) on dry samples in the range 10–80. Emission spectra was recorded using a F-7000 Hitachi Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. Size of carbon dots (CDs) was studied by JEOL-JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. Preparation of sample for the TEM study was done by placing one drop sample sol. onto a film of carbon supported via copper grid followed by solvent evaporation under vacuum. Zeta potential and size distribution of the particle were measured by using the instrument Malvern Zeta-sizer Nano ZEN3600. FTIR measurement was done with a PerkinElmer (Spectrum RX1) spectrophotometer following the KBr disk technique. NMR (1H, 13C, HSQC and HMBC) (300 MHz) spectra were studied on a BRUKER-AC 300 MHz spectrometer. The X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) of the carbon dots (CDs) was measured by a JPS-9030, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan. Transient fluorescence measurements were carried out using a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) spectrometer [a picosecond diode laser (IBH, UK)] under ambient conditions with the detection wavelength at 450 nm for the carbon dots (CDs) solution and the wavelength of excitation was 288 nm for the samples.

Dioscorea alata, also known as purple yam (local name khamalu) cultivated in Southeast Asia was purchased from the local market, Rajabazar, Midnapore. Crystal violet (CV) (Sigma-Aldrich) and Conc.H2SO4 were procured from S. D. Fine Chemicals and used as received. Milli-Q-purified deionized (DI) water is used throughout the entire experiments. The total glassware was washed thoroughly with aqua regia and then with distilled water before the start of the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation, purification and structural characterization of the DAPS
ce:section.ce:para: The crude polysaccharide was isolated from aqueous extract of Diascorea Alata by using ethanol precipitation, dialysis, centrifugation at very low temperature, and freeze-drying. 25 mg of crude polysaccharide on fractionation (Scheme-1) through Sepharose-6B column produced single homogeneous pure water-soluble polysaccharide, DAPS (16 mg). The DAPS showed specific rotation [α]D 28 +171 (c 0.24, H2O). The average molecular weight of DAPS was estimated as ∼1.2 × 105 Da.

The Gerwig et al. Method [36] and GLC analysis of the alditol acetates [37] showed that d-glucose was only the monosaccharide moiety of DAPS. The GLC-MS analysis of alditol acetates of methylated product revealed the presence of 1,4,5-tri-O-acetyl-2,3,6-tri-O-methyl-D-glucitol and 1,4,5-tri-O-acetyl-2,3,6-tri-O-methyl-D-glucitol in a molar ratio of nearly 1:1. These results were consistent with the result of periodate oxidation and indicated that DAPS contained (1 → 4) and (1 → 6)-linked d-glucose moieties.

Two anomeric signals at 5.38 and 4.95 ppm was obtained in the 1H NMR spectrum (500 MHz; Fig. 1 a, Table 1 ) at 30 °C and these were designated as A and B (Table 1). At the same temperature two anomeric signals have appeared in the region at 100.0 and 98.0 ppm in the 13C NMR spectrum (125 MHz; Fig. 1b). The anomeric carbons at 100.0 and 98.0 ppm were correlated to the anomeric protons at 5.38 and 4.95 ppm respectively in HSQC spectrum (Fig. 2 a; Table 1). All the 1H and 13C signals (Table 1) were assigned using TOCSY, DQF-COSY, and HSQC experiments. From DQF-COSY experiment, the proton coupling constants were measured.

Anomeric proton and carbon chemical shifts (at δ5.38, 4.95; 100.0, 98.0 ppm) as well as J H-1,H-2 ∼2.0 Hz and J H-1,C-1 ∼172.0 Hz confirmed the presence of residues A and B in α-configuration [38]. Presence of residues A and B as d-glucopyranosyl configuration (Glcp) were confirmed by the large J H-2,H-3 and J H-3,H-4 coupling constant values (∼10.0 Hz). The downfield shift of C-4 (77.1 ppm) of residue A and C-4 (66.0 ppm) of residue B with respect to their standard value methyl glucosides [39,40] indicated that residue A and B were present as (1 → 4) and (1 → 6)-linked α-D-Glcp respectively.

From HMBC (Fig. 2b–Table 2 ) experiment, the determination of the different linkages that connected these residues (A and B) were done. In this spectrum, the inter-residual cross-peaks (Table 2) between AH-1/BC-6, AC-1/BH-6a, AC-1/AB-6b; BH-1/AC-4, BC-1/AH-4 along with some intra-residual peaks were showed in Fig. 2b. Thus, the HMBC connectivity clearly supported the presence of the following repeating unit in the DAPS and the structure is proposed as:
A
B
→ 4 ) − α − D − Glc p
( 1 → 6 )
− α − D − Glc p ( 1 →

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and compositional descriptions of as-prepared carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Typical TEM measurement was significant way (displayed in Fig. 3 a) for the identification of the morphology and structure of the synthesized carbon dots (CDs). TEM image indicates about the spherical shape of carbon dots (CDs) having diameter ∼3–4 nm without any apparent aggregation. The carbon dots (CDs) obtained is of 3.28 nm average sizes, depicted in the bar diagram (Fig. 3b). More investigations were done to get more information about the synthesized carbon dots (CDs) particle. Fig. 3c gives the size-distribution of the carbon dots (CDs) established by Malvern Zetasizer ZEN3600. From DLS measurement, the average particle size of carbon dots (CDs) was approximated to ∼4–5 nm which is slightly larger than TEM measurement. This larger value is due to hydrodynamic diameter of the nanoparticles. Further, the measurement of the zeta potential was done to identify the net surface charge that carbon dots (CDs) have. The zeta value obtained was −23.5 mV which ensures that negatively charged moieties were present on the carbon dots (CDs) surface. It was seen that carbon dots (CDs) solution showed no cloudiness even after 1 month. This phenomenon gives us assurance about the good colloidal stability of carbon dots (CDs). Fig. 3e represents the zeta potential distribution. In order to be familiar with the chemical composition of carbon dots (CDs), EDX analysis (as shown in Fig. 3d) is done. It has been found that the carbon dots (CDs) contain carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in the % of 56.3, 5 and 38.7 respectively.

For the characterization of carbon dots (CDs), UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy studies were done. A peak at approximately 285 nm (as shown in Fig. 4 a) is obtained from the absorption spectra of carbon dots (CDs). This peak is due to π-π∗ transition of –CC– bond (of aromatic ring) in the sp2 hybridized area on the carbon dots (CDs) surface [41]. It is imagined that the glucan polysaccharide having glucose units turned into polymers by hydrothermal process using microwave-assisted technique and finally to carbon dots (CDs). Fluorescence spectroscopy profile of the carbon dots (CDs) is displayed in Fig. 4b. The emission wavelength reached maxima at 368 nm with an excitation wavelength of 280 nm. The exact mechanism of fluorescence which is generated by the carbon dots (CDs) is debatable even now. Mainly it is owing to three reasons (i) quantum-confinement effect, (ii) surface-defects states, and (iii) molecular state fluorescence. Zhao et al. [42] demanded that the origin of fluorescence was due to differences in size rather than trap states in particles of almost similar size for the tunable optical emission of carbon dots (CDs). On the other hand, another mechanism for fluorescence emission in carbon dots (CDs) is suggested based on surface defect states. Sun et al. claimed that carbon dots (CDs) show strong fluorescence by passivation on surface due to the quantum-confinement effect [43]. Here the fluorescence origin of carbon dots (CDs) may be due to the surface of the particles, precisely “surface defects” liable to light absorbance in particular wavelengths. Furthermore, Time-correlated Single-photon Counting (TCSPC) experiments for synthesized carbon dots (CDs) are done. In TCSPC experiment, the time delay between excitation and emission is measured. Fluorescence lifetime, the time a molecule spends in the excited state, can be calculated by plotting fluorescence intensity versus time (shown in Fig. 4c). It is seen that the lifetime of carbon dots (CDs) is 494 ps (99 %) and 6.3 ns (1 %) which clearly indicates the fluorescence mechanism of green luminescent carbon dots (CDs) following the radiative recombination process. The quantum yield of the synthesized carbon dots (CDs) obtained from fluorescence studies is 27.70 %. The effects of UV and visible light on the luminescence characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) were investigated by using UV chamber. The carbon dots (CDs) aqueous solution emitted green color fluorescence under UV light (approx. 365 nm) while appearing white under day light (shown in Fig. 4d).

The compositional information of the carbon dots (CDs) is inspected by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Characteristic peaks of C 1s, O 1s and N 1s is seen in XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 5 a). From XPS, the atomic % of the element on the surface of carbon dots (CDs) obtained is C = 56.3 %, O = 38.7 %, N = 5 % which supports EDX result. The high-resolution XP spectrum of C 1s is shown in Fig. 5b, where the bands are observed at 283.9, 285.0, 286.8, and 289.0 eV binding energy that corresponds to the CC [44], C–C [44], C–O [45], and CO [46] respectively. The deconvoluted core level XPS spectrum of O 1s indicates two peaks as given in Fig. 5c. The peak at 529.9 eV related to the C–O–C/C–OH and 531.7 eV suggests O–CO bands respectively [47]. The high-resolution XP spectrum of N 1s in Fig. 5d shows two peaks at 401.1 and 402.6 eV for C–NH–C and –NH2 respectively [47]. The observed data is comparable to the recently published carbon dots (CDs) related materials that indicates successful preparation of the materials [44,47,48].

Presence of the groups on the carbon dots (CDs) surface were analyzed by the FT-IR study. The FTIR provide the FTIR spectra of carbon dots (CDs) (Fig. 6 ). FTIR spectra of DAPS displayed a broad peak at 3372 cm-1 belongs to stretching vibration O–H/N–H. The presence of CO bond was confirmed by a peak at 1646 cm-1 and the peaks at 1348, 1166, 1049, 875 cm-1 reflect the functional groups, carboxylate COO-, hydroxyl C–OH, epoxide C–O–C and –CH vibration respectively [49]. Owing to the presence of these groups, the carbon dots (CDs) were excellently soluble in water-based solvent and its’ zeta potential value becomes negative [50].

XRD analysis again confirms the existence of carbon dots (CDs) (Fig. 7 ). The synthesized carbon dots (CDs) displayed a peak (broad) at 23.34⁰ which corresponds to the hkl (002) plane. The interlayer spacing d between 002 planes of carbon dots (CDs) is higher than the inter-layer spacing in perfect graphite. A higher value of d indicates a turbostratic phase of carbon [51]. So, the prepared carbon dots (CDs) are of poor crystalline nature.

To check how much stability the carbon dots (CDs) have, fluorescence intensity was studied by using a Fluorimeter (Hitachi-7000) after 5 days–30 days on a time interval of 5 days (Fig. 8 d) and on irradiation of UV light, 20 min–120 min a time interval of 20 min (Fig. 8c). It is seen from the result that fluorescence intensity remained unchanged. This is a sign of good stability of carbon dots (CDs). In addition, the stability study of carbon dots (CDs) was also performed at PH 1 to 14 (Fig. 8a) and using NaCl salt solution of concentrations 0.1 M–1 M (Fig. 8b). carbon dots (CDs) showed sensitivity towards PH 2 in fluorescence emission study and fluorescence intensity remained nearly constant at PH 3 to 14. The exact reason for PH sensitive fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) may be for protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups that contains oxygen at the surface of carbon dots (CDs). Zhao et al. [52] demanded that at lower PH the hydrogen bonding effect could result to the powerful vibronic coupling of –OH that could lead to broadening of energy levels and enhance the conformational rigidness of carbon dots (CDs). That's why strong fluorescence intensity was seen at lower PH. However, at higher PH (alkaline condition), the hydrogen bonding effect was eliminated through the progress in deprotonation and there was reduction in vibronic coupling. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots (CDs) was reduced at higher PH. And also by using NaCl salt solution of concentrations 0.1 M–1 M fluorescence intensity remained the same. From the result, it is clear that carbon dots (CDs) possessed good stability. So, these carbon dots (CDs) can be applied in the dye degradation process successfully due to their high stability.

The catalytic activity of the carbon dots (CDs) was examined in terms of the photocatalytic degradation of the CV, a triphenylmethane dye, using an aqueous solution under sunlight. The catalytic performance was analyzed by using UV–vis spectra at 625 nm wavelength, fluorescence spectroscopy and also through visual observation. Fig. 9 a & b show the catalyst's influence on the degradation of 10 ppm solution of CV dye when kept under the sunlight (in catalyst's presence and absence). In dark condition, the catalyst has no effect. But in the presence of sunlight, degradation of CV dye occurred with the catalyst in 8 min. Fig. 9a shows the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of the CV in catalyst's presence and Fig. 9b shows the emission profile of CV aqueous solutions in catalyst's presence at different degradation times. Furthermore, % of degradation and reaction rate as per kinetic model for CV were calculated. The degradation levels achieved for the CV dye were about 99.9 % in 8 min. Degradation time vs. ln (At/A0) plot of kinetics was shown in Fig. 9c. It is seen that the kinetic plot of CV gave a linear straight-line having rate const. of 0.32861 min−1 and the corresponding value of linearity coefficient (R2) was 0.9919. From the result, it is clear that the photo-degradation of CV by carbon dots (CDs) obeyed the kinetics of pseudo 1st order reaction. Photographic images represent the degradation pathway of aqueous solution of CV in catalyst's [carbon dots (CDs)] presence. The aqueous solution of CV in sunlight turned into colourless (transparent solution in the right cuvette) from violet colour (left side violet cuvette) by the effect of carbon dots (CDs) as shown in Fig. 9d.

Here, % of degradation determination was done according to equation (Eq. (2))
(2) % Degradation = ((At- A0)/ A0) × 100%
Where At is the time t absorbance of CV, A0 is time zero absorbance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of dye degradation by using synthesized carbon dots (CDs) as photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of dye degradation is explained in Scheme 2 . On light irradiation upon the catalyst, photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants happened. Here we used the carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from DAPS as photocatalysts without doping of any heteroatom or amalgamation of any semiconductor nanosubstance's. carbon dots (CDs) photocatalytic performance was examined in terms of degradation of toxic CV dye. Here on absorbing photons of appropriate energy, carbon dots (CDs) were excited. As a result, electrons were keyed up from the filled VB to the CB. Electron-hole pair is generated due to development of positive holes (h+) in the VB and free electron (e−) in the CB. These holes and electrons joined together for the production of thermal energy. Alternatively, these can also move towards the catalyst for reacting with the adsorbed molecules. Actually, the valence band's holes will take part in the reaction with water for hydroxyl radical production. On the other hand, the CB electrons in reaction with oxygen formed the reactive species of oxygen or superoxide [23]. The reactive species obtained will further participate in the dye degradation process. In our present study, the dye degradation activity of synthesized carbon dots (CDs) on toxic CV dye was studied by change observation visually, through UV–vis spectroscopy and emission spectroscopy.

Here we described the isolation and structural characterization of (1 → 4), (1 → 6)-α-D-glucan from the aqueous extracting of the Diascorea Alata. Different characterization approaches were used for structure determination of pure polysaccharide (1 → 4), (1 → 6)-α-D-glucan (DAPS). The extremely fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with high quantum yield (27.70 %) were prepared using environmentally benign pure polysaccharide α-D-glucan (DAPS) as a carbon source in a green synthetic route (microwave assisted method). The synthesized carbon dots (CDs) exhibit green fluorescence and good stability such that their FL intensity remained unchanged even under PH change or after one month. For massive scale manufacture of carbon dots (CDs), this technique is facile and achievable. The effective photoactive synthesized carbon dots (CDs) can be used in sunlight stimulated photo-degradation of CV dye successfully. This dye degradation procedure would have more acceptances for being facile, green, fast, less hazardous and affordable that makes it suitable for waste water treatment.

Arnab Mallik: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Sukesh Patra: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Animesh Patra: Software, Methodology, Investigation. Akhil Pandey: Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis. Madhumita Hazra: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Gobinda Prasad Sahoo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Gobinda Prasad Sahoo reports was provided by Midnapore College (Autonomous). If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The instruments facilities obtained from the economic support of Rashtriya Uchchatar Shiksha Abhiyan (RUSA) (Ref. No. 620/MC/RUSA2.0/PROJECT/19/13(11); Dated: March 14, 2019), MHRD, Department of Higher Education, Govt. of India, New Delhi and Research Center (Science), Midnapore College (Autonomous) to carry out the investigation is gratefully acknowledged. We are thankful to Prof. Ajay Misra, Dept. of Chemistry & Chemical Tech., Vidyasagar University, Midnapore, W. B., India, for allowing us to do the TCSPC measurement.

Yim, Green synthesis of multicolour fluorescence carbon quantum dots from sugarcane waste: Investigation of mercury (II) ion sensing, and bio-imaging applications, Appl. Surf. Sci. 601 (2022) 154266, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.154266.
